verses.explainer: Daniel heard a holy one ask: 'How long will the vision be -- the daily sacrifice abolished, the rebellion that causes desolation, the surrender of the sanctuary and the trampling of the host?' The question is about duration: how long will this desecration last?

verses.text: He said to me, “To two thousand and three hundred evenings and mornings. Then the sanctuary will be cleansed.”
verses.explainer: The answer: 2,300 evenings and mornings, then the sanctuary will be reconsecrated. If this refers to 2,300 individual sacrifices (two per day = 1,150 days, about three years and two months), it closely matches the historical period of Antiochus's desecration (167-164 BC).

culturalNotes.title: The Reconsecration
culturalNotes.note: The reconsecration of the Temple after Antiochus's desecration is celebrated as Hanukkah (the Festival of Dedication). The Maccabean revolt succeeded in liberating Jerusalem, cleansing the Temple, and restoring proper worship -- fulfilling this prophecy.

crossReferences.connection: Jesus was in Jerusalem for the Festival of Dedication (Hanukkah) -- the celebration of the Temple's reconsecration prophesied here.

verses.text: When I, even I Daniel, had seen the vision, I sought to understand it. Then behold, there stood before me something like the appearance of a man.
verses.explainer: As Daniel tried to understand the vision, someone who looked like a man stood before him. An angelic figure appears to provide interpretation.

verses.text: I heard a man’s voice between the banks of the Ulai, which called, and said, “Gabriel, make this man understand the vision.”
verses.explainer: Daniel heard a man's voice from the Ulai canal calling out: 'Gabriel, tell this man the meaning of the vision.' This is the first time an angel is named in the Bible. Gabriel appears again in chapter 9 and later in Luke's Gospel.

crossReferences.connection: Gabriel told Zechariah: 'I am Gabriel. I stand in the presence of God, and I have been sent to speak to you.'
Gabriel was sent to Mary to announce the birth of Jesus.

verses.text: So he came near where I stood; and when he came, I was frightened, and fell on my face; but he said to me, “Understand, son of man; for the vision belongs to the time of the end.”
verses.explainer: Gabriel approached Daniel and told him the vision concerns 'the time of the end.' Daniel was terrified and fell face down. The phrase 'time of the end' suggests the vision has implications beyond the immediate historical fulfillment.

verses.text: Now as he was speaking with me, I fell into a deep sleep with my face toward the ground; but he touched me, and set me upright.
verses.explainer: While Gabriel was speaking, Daniel fell into a deep sleep face down on the ground. The angel touched him and raised him to his feet. The physical effect of angelic encounter is overwhelming.

crossReferences.connection: John also 'fell at his feet as though dead' when he encountered the risen Christ.

verses.text: He said, “Behold, I will make you know what will be in the latter time of the indignation; for it belongs to the appointed time of the end.
verses.explainer: Gabriel says he will tell Daniel what will happen in the latter time of wrath, for it concerns the appointed time of the end. The emphasis on 'appointed time' means these events are part of God's plan, not random.

verses.text: The ram which you saw, that had the two horns, they are the kings of Media and Persia.
verses.explainer: The two-horned ram represents the kings of Media and Persia. The identification is explicit -- no need for speculation.

verses.text: The rough male goat is the king of Greece. The great horn that is between his eyes is the first king.
verses.explainer: The shaggy goat is the king of Greece, and the large horn is the first king (Alexander). The identifications are remarkably specific.

verses.text: As for that which was broken, in the place where four stood up, four kingdoms will stand up out of the nation, but not with his power.
verses.explainer: The four horns that replaced the broken one are four kingdoms that will emerge from his nation but will not have his power. Alexander's successors inherited his territory but not his military genius.

crossReferences.connection: The same four-way division described with additional detail.

verses.text: “In the latter time of their kingdom, when the transgressors have come to the full, a king of fierce face, and understanding dark sentences, will stand up.
verses.explainer: In the latter part of their reign, when rebels have become completely wicked, a fierce-looking king skilled in intrigue will arise. This describes Antiochus IV Epiphanes, who gained power through political manipulation.

culturalNotes.title: Antiochus IV Epiphanes
culturalNotes.note: Antiochus IV ruled the Seleucid Empire from 175-164 BC. He gave himself the title 'Epiphanes' ('God Manifest'), though his opponents called him 'Epimanes' ('the Madman'). He attempted to forcibly Hellenize the Jews, banning circumcision, Torah study, and Temple sacrifices, and erecting a pagan altar in the Temple.

verses.text: His power will be mighty, but not by his own power. He will destroy awesomely, and will prosper in what he does. He will destroy the mighty ones and the holy people.
verses.explainer: He will become very strong -- but not by his own power. He will cause astounding devastation and destroy the mighty and the holy people. The phrase 'not by his own power' may mean he is empowered by demonic forces or simply by political cunning rather than military might.

crossReferences.connection: The historical account of Antiochus's first attack on Jerusalem and the Temple.

verses.text: Through his policy he will cause deceit to prosper in his hand. He will magnify himself in his heart, and he will destroy many in their security. He will also stand up against the prince of princes; but he will be broken without hand.
verses.explainer: Through his cunning, he will cause deceit to prosper. He will consider himself superior and destroy many who feel secure. He will take his stand against the Prince of princes, but he will be destroyed -- not by human power.

crossReferences.connection: Antiochus died of a painful disease during a military campaign -- 'destroyed, but not by human power.'

verses.text: “The vision of the evenings and mornings which has been told is true; but seal up the vision, for it belongs to many days to come.”
verses.explainer: The vision of the evenings and mornings is true, but Daniel is told to seal it up because it concerns the distant future. The events described were centuries away from Daniel's time.

crossReferences.connection: Daniel is again told to seal up the prophecy because it concerns the end times.

verses.text: I, Daniel, fainted, and was sick for some days. Then I rose up, and did the king’s business. I wondered at the vision, but no one understood it.
verses.explainer: Daniel was exhausted and lay ill for several days. Then he got up and went about the king's business. He was appalled by the vision and did not understand it. The weight of prophetic revelation took a physical toll.

thenVsNow.then: The physical and emotional cost of receiving divine revelation is consistently described in Scripture. Encountering God's plans is overwhelming, not casual.
thenVsNow.now: The physical and emotional cost of receiving divine revelation is consistently described in Scripture. Encountering God's plans is overwhelming, not casual.

explainer.summary: Daniel reads Jeremiah's prophecy that the exile would last 70 years and realizes the time is nearly up. He responds with one of the greatest prayers in the Bible -- a passionate confession of sin and plea for mercy. The angel Gabriel then delivers the famous 'Seventy Weeks' prophecy, outlining a timeline from the decree to rebuild Jerusalem through the coming of the Messiah and ultimately to the end.
themes: The prophetic timeline to the Messiah
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 9 connects Daniel's prophetic visions to the concrete historical promise of return from exile. It also provides the most explicit messianic timeline in the Old Testament, linking the rebuilding of Jerusalem to the coming of 'Messiah the Prince.'

wordStudies.meaning: Seventy sevens or seventy weeks. Most interpreters understand these as 'weeks of years' -- seventy periods of seven years each, totaling 490 years.
wordStudies.significance: The 'seventy weeks' provide a prophetic timeline from the decree to rebuild Jerusalem to the coming of the Messiah and the final events of history.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The anointed one, from which the title 'Messiah' d
wordStudies.meaning: The anointed one, from which the title 'Messiah' derives. Originally referred to anyone anointed for a special role (kings, priests), but here used as a title for the ultimate deliverer.
wordStudies.significance: The 'Anointed One' who is 'cut off' (v.26) is one of the most important messianic prophecies in the Old Testament, interpreted by Christians as pointing directly to Jesus's crucifixion.

verses.text: In the first year of Darius the son of Ahasuerus, of the offspring of the Medes, who was made king over the realm of the Chaldeans,
verses.explainer: In the first year of Darius (539 BC), Daniel understood from Jeremiah's writings that Jerusalem's desolation would last seventy years. Daniel's prayer life was informed by Scripture -- he read the prophets and responded with prayer.

crossReferences.connection: 'These nations will serve the king of Babylon seventy years. But when the seventy years are fulfilled, I will punish the king of Babylon.'
'When seventy years are completed for Babylon, I will come to you and fulfill my good promise to bring you back to this place.'

verses.text: in the first year of his reign I, Daniel, understood by the books the number of the years about which Yahweh’s word came to Jeremiah the prophet, for the accomplishing of the desolations of Jerusalem, even seventy years.
verses.explainer: Daniel turned to the Lord God and pleaded with Him in prayer and petition, with fasting, sackcloth, and ashes. His response to reading Scripture was not passive acceptance but active, passionate prayer.

culturalNotes.title: Sackcloth and Ashes
culturalNotes.note: Wearing rough sackcloth (burlap-like fabric) and sitting in ashes were traditional Jewish expressions of mourning, repentance, and humility before God. They signified that the person recognized their lowly state before God.

verses.text: I set my face to the Lord God, to seek by prayer and petitions, with fasting and sackcloth and ashes.
verses.explainer: Daniel prayed to the Lord his God, making confession. Despite being personally righteous (even his enemies could find no fault in him), Daniel identifies with his nation's sin. This is corporate confession.

verses.text:
I prayed to Yahweh my God, and made confession, and said,
“Oh, Lord, the great and dreadful God, who keeps covenant and loving kindness with those who love him and keep his commandments,
verses.explainer: Daniel addresses God as great and awesome, who keeps His covenant of love with those who love Him and keep His commandments. He begins with God's character before confessing sin.

crossReferences.connection: 'Know therefore that the Lord your God is God; he is the faithful God, keeping his covenant of love to a thousand generations.'

verses.text: we have sinned, and have dealt perversely, and have done wickedly, and have rebelled, even turning aside from your precepts and from your ordinances.
verses.explainer: We have sinned, done wrong, been wicked, rebelled, and turned away from your commands and laws. Daniel uses five different words for sin, emphasizing its comprehensive nature. He says 'we,' not 'they.'

crossReferences.connection: 'If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just and will forgive us our sins.'

verses.text: We haven’t listened to your servants the prophets, who spoke in your name to our kings, our princes, and our fathers, and to all the people of the land.
verses.explainer: We have not listened to your servants the prophets who spoke in your name to our kings, princes, ancestors, and all the people of the land. The nation's root sin was ignoring God's messengers.

crossReferences.connection: 'The Lord sent word to them through his messengers again and again, because he had pity on his people. But they mocked God's messengers, despised his words and scoffed at his prophets.'

verses.text: “Lord, righteousness belongs to you, but to us confusion of face, as it is today; to the men of Judah, and to the inhabitants of Jerusalem, and to all Israel, who are near, and who are far off, through all the countries where you have driven them, because of their trespass that they have trespassed against you.
verses.explainer: Righteousness belongs to You, Lord, but to us, shame -- to the men of Judah, the people of Jerusalem, and all Israel. Daniel acknowledges that God is right and the people are guilty.

crossReferences.connection: 'The Lord is righteous, for I rebelled against his commandment.'

verses.text: Lord, to us belongs confusion of face, to our kings, to our princes, and to our fathers, because we have sinned against you.
verses.explainer: To our kings, princes, and ancestors belongs shame because we have sinned against You. Daniel does not excuse leadership -- they share the guilt.

verses.text: To the Lord our God belong mercies and forgiveness; for we have rebelled against him.
verses.explainer: To the Lord our God belong mercy and forgiveness, though we have rebelled against Him. Even in confession, Daniel anchors hope in God's character: He is a God of mercy.

crossReferences.connection: 'The Lord, the Lord, the compassionate and gracious God, slow to anger, abounding in love and faithfulness.'

verses.text: We haven’t obeyed Yahweh our God’s voice, to walk in his laws, which he set before us by his servants the prophets.
verses.explainer: We have not obeyed the Lord our God or kept the laws He gave us through His servants the prophets. The confession returns to the specific sin: disobedience.

verses.text:
Yes, all Israel have transgressed your law, turning aside, that they should not obey your voice.
“Therefore the curse and the oath written in the law of Moses the servant of God has been poured out on us; for we have sinned against him.
verses.explainer: All Israel has transgressed your law and turned away, refusing to obey. Therefore the curses and oaths written in the Law of Moses have been poured out on us. The exile was not random but the fulfillment of specific covenant warnings.

crossReferences.connection: 'If you do not obey the Lord your God and do not carefully follow all his commands, all these curses will come on you.'

verses.text: He has confirmed his words, which he spoke against us, and against our judges who judged us, by bringing on us a great evil; for under the whole sky, such has not been done as has been done to Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: God carried out His warnings against us and our rulers by bringing great disaster on us. Nothing like what has happened to Jerusalem has ever happened under all of heaven.

verses.text: As it is written in the law of Moses, all this evil has come on us. Yet have we not entreated the favor of Yahweh our God, that we should turn from our iniquities, and have discernment in your truth.
verses.explainer: Just as it is written in the Law of Moses, all this disaster has come on us, yet we have not sought the favor of the Lord our God by turning from our sins and giving attention to His truth.

crossReferences.connection: God promised that if they confess their sins and the sins of their ancestors, He will remember His covenant.

verses.text: Therefore Yahweh has watched over the evil, and brought it on us; for Yahweh our God is righteous in all his works which he does, and we have not obeyed his voice.
verses.explainer: The Lord kept the disaster ready and brought it on us because He is righteous in everything He does, yet we did not obey Him. Daniel affirms God's justice even in bringing disaster.

verses.text: “Now, Lord our God, who has brought your people out of the land of Egypt with a mighty hand, and have gotten yourself renown, as it is today; we have sinned. We have done wickedly.
verses.explainer: Daniel appeals to the Exodus: You brought Your people out of Egypt with a mighty hand and made a name for Yourself. But we have sinned. He is reminding God of His past acts of salvation.

crossReferences.connection: The Exodus -- God's greatest act of deliverance in the Old Testament -- is invoked as the basis for hope.

verses.text: Lord, according to all your righteousness, let your anger and please let your wrath be turned away from your city Jerusalem, your holy mountain; because for our sins, and for the iniquities of our fathers, Jerusalem and your people have become a reproach to all who are around us.
verses.explainer: Daniel asks God to turn away His anger from Jerusalem, His holy hill. Because of their sins and the iniquities of their ancestors, Jerusalem and God's people are disgraced before all their neighbors.

verses.text: “Now therefore, our God, listen to the prayer of your servant, and to his petitions, and cause your face to shine on your sanctuary that is desolate, for the Lord’s sake.
verses.explainer: Hear the prayers and petitions of your servant. For your sake, Lord, look with favor on your desolate sanctuary. Daniel prays 'for your sake' -- God's reputation is tied to Jerusalem's fate.

verses.text: My God, turn your ear, and hear. Open your eyes, and see our desolations, and the city which is called by your name; for we do not present our petitions before you for our righteousness, but for your great mercies’ sake.
verses.explainer: We do not make requests because of our righteousness but because of your great mercy. Daniel does not claim the people deserve restoration -- they do not. He appeals entirely to God's compassion.

crossReferences.connection: 'He saved us, not because of righteous things we had done, but because of his mercy.'

verses.text: Lord, hear. Lord, forgive. Lord, listen and do. Don’t defer, for your own sake, my God, because your city and your people are called by your name.”
verses.explainer: Lord, listen! Lord, forgive! Lord, hear and act! For your sake, my God, do not delay, because your city and your people bear your Name. The prayer reaches its most intense, urgent pitch.

thenVsNow.then: Daniel's prayer is a model of bold, urgent, Scripture-informed intercession. He does not passively wait for God to act but actively pleads.
thenVsNow.now: Daniel's prayer is a model of bold, urgent, Scripture-informed intercession. He does not passively wait for God to act but actively pleads.

verses.text: While I was speaking, and praying, and confessing my sin and the sin of my people Israel, and presenting my supplication before Yahweh my God for the holy mountain of my God;
verses.explainer: While Daniel was still praying, confessing sin and making his request for God's holy hill, the answer came before the prayer was finished.

verses.text: yes, while I was speaking in prayer, the man Gabriel, whom I had seen in the vision at the beginning, being caused to fly swiftly, touched me about the time of the evening offering.
verses.explainer: Gabriel, the angel Daniel had seen in the earlier vision, came to him in swift flight at about the time of the evening sacrifice. This is the same Gabriel who later announced Jesus's birth to Mary.

crossReferences.connection: Gabriel was first identified by name in Daniel's previous vision.
Gabriel was sent to Nazareth to announce to Mary that she would bear the Messiah.

verses.text: He instructed me, and talked with me, and said, Daniel, “I have now come to give you wisdom and understanding.
verses.explainer: Gabriel said he had come to give Daniel insight and understanding. Daniel is called 'greatly loved' -- a title of deep divine affection.

verses.text: At the beginning of your petitions the commandment went out, and I have come to tell you; for you are greatly beloved. Therefore consider the matter, and understand the vision.
verses.explainer: As soon as Daniel began to pray, a word went out and Gabriel came to tell him. God responded to the prayer immediately, even though the answer took time to arrive.

thenVsNow.then: The principle that God responds to prayer immediately -- even when the answer takes time to arrive -- is encouraging for anyone who wonders if God hears.
thenVsNow.now: The principle that God responds to prayer immediately -- even when the answer takes time to arrive -- is encouraging for anyone who wonders if God hears.

verses.text: “Seventy weeks are decreed on your people and on your holy city, to finish disobedience, and to make an end of sins, and to make reconciliation for iniquity, and to bring in everlasting righteousness, and to seal up vision and prophecy, and to anoint the most holy.
verses.explainer: Seventy 'weeks' (490 years) are decreed for Daniel's people and holy city: to finish transgression, put an end to sin, atone for wickedness, bring in everlasting righteousness, seal up vision and prophecy, and anoint the Most Holy Place. This is one of the most debated and important prophecies in the Bible.

crossReferences.connection: Christ's atonement accomplished several of the goals listed here: putting an end to sin and bringing in everlasting righteousness.

verses.text: “Know therefore and discern that from the going out of the commandment to restore and to build Jerusalem to the Anointed One, the prince, will be seven weeks and sixty-two weeks. It will be built again, with street and moat, even in troubled times.
verses.explainer: From the issuing of the decree to restore and rebuild Jerusalem until the Anointed One (Messiah) the ruler comes, there will be seven 'weeks' and sixty-two 'weeks.' Jerusalem will be rebuilt with streets and a trench in times of trouble.

culturalNotes.title: The Seventy Weeks Timeline
culturalNotes.note: Starting from the decree to rebuild Jerusalem (usually dated to 445 BC under Artaxerxes, per Nehemiah 2), 69 'weeks' (483 years) would reach approximately 30-33 AD -- precisely the time of Jesus's public ministry. This mathematical precision has made this passage one of the most discussed prophecies in Jewish and Christian history.

crossReferences.connection: The decree of Artaxerxes allowing Nehemiah to rebuild Jerusalem's walls -- often identified as the starting point of the seventy weeks.

verses.text: After the sixty-two weeks the Anointed One will be cut off, and will have nothing. The people of the prince who come will destroy the city and the sanctuary. Its end will be with a flood, and war will be even to the end. Desolations are determined.
verses.explainer: After the sixty-two 'weeks,' the Anointed One will be 'cut off' and will have nothing. The people of the ruler who will come will destroy the city and the sanctuary. The end will come like a flood. Christians identify the Anointed One 'cut off' as Jesus's crucifixion, and the destruction of the city as the Roman destruction of Jerusalem in 70 AD.

crossReferences.connection: 'By oppression and judgment he was taken away... he was cut off from the land of the living.'
Jesus predicted the Temple's destruction: 'Not one stone here will be left on another.'

thenVsNow.then: The prophecy that the Messiah would be 'cut off' (killed) before the destruction of the Temple is one of the strongest messianic arguments in the Old Testament.
thenVsNow.now: The prophecy that the Messiah would be 'cut off' (killed) before the destruction of the Temple is one of the strongest messianic arguments in the Old Testament.

verses.text: He will make a firm covenant with many for one week. In the middle of the week he will cause the sacrifice and the offering to cease. On the wing of abominations will come one who makes desolate; and even to the full end, and that determined, wrath will be poured out on the desolate.”
verses.explainer: He will confirm a covenant with many for one 'week.' In the middle of the 'week,' he will put an end to sacrifice and offering. And at the temple, he will set up an abomination that causes desolation, until the end that is decreed is poured out on him. The final 'week' (7 years) is the most debated section of biblical prophecy.

crossReferences.connection: Jesus said: 'When you see standing in the holy place the abomination that causes desolation spoken of through the prophet Daniel, then let those who are in Judea flee.'
Paul describes a 'man of lawlessness' who sets himself up in God's temple -- possibly the fulfillment of this abomination.

thenVsNow.then: Daniel 9:27 is the foundation for much end-times theology. The concepts of a final seven-year period, the abomination of desolation, and a covenant-breaking ruler all stem from this single verse.
thenVsNow.now: Daniel 9:27 is the foundation for much end-times theology. The concepts of a final seven-year period, the abomination of desolation, and a covenant-breaking ruler all stem from this single verse.

explainer.summary: In the third year of Cyrus (536 BC), Daniel receives a final, overwhelming vision. He mourns and fasts for three weeks before an angelic figure appears to him in dazzling glory -- so overwhelming that Daniel collapses. The angel reveals that he was delayed 21 days by the 'prince of Persia' (a spiritual power) until Michael came to help. This chapter pulls back the curtain on spiritual warfare happening behind the scenes of world history.
themes: The overwhelming nature of divine revelation
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 10 is the introduction to the final vision that spans chapters 10-12. It provides the spiritual context: behind the political events described in chapters 11-12, a cosmic spiritual battle is raging.

wordStudies.meaning: A ruler, chief, or commander. In this context, it refers to powerful angelic or demonic beings who exercise spiritual authority over nations.
wordStudies.significance: The 'prince of Persia' and 'prince of Greece' are spiritual beings (likely fallen angels) who exert influence over these empires. Michael is called 'your prince' -- the angelic protector of Israel.

wordStudies.meaning: Precious, treasured, greatly desired. This is God's term of affection for Daniel.
wordStudies.significance: Three times in this chapter (vv.11, 19), Daniel is called 'greatly loved' or 'treasured' -- an extraordinary expression of divine affection for a human being.

verses.text: In the third year of Cyrus king of Persia a thing was revealed to Daniel, whose name was called Belteshazzar; and the thing was true, even a great warfare. He understood the thing, and had understanding of the vision.
verses.explainer: In the third year of Cyrus king of Persia, a revelation was given to Daniel. Its message was true and it concerned a great war. Daniel understood the vision through a subsequent revelation.

verses.text: In those days I, Daniel, was mourning three whole weeks.
verses.explainer: Daniel mourned for three full weeks -- eating no rich food, no meat or wine, and using no lotions. This extended fast preceded the vision, suggesting that serious spiritual revelation sometimes requires serious spiritual preparation.

crossReferences.connection: Jesus said some spiritual breakthroughs require prayer and fasting.

verses.text: I ate no pleasant bread. No meat or wine came into my mouth. I didn’t anoint myself at all, until three whole weeks were fulfilled.
verses.explainer: He ate no delicacies for the full 21 days. This 'Daniel fast' of abstaining from pleasant food while continuing to eat basic sustenance has become a widely practiced form of fasting.

thenVsNow.then: The 'Daniel Fast' is one of the most commonly practiced fasts in churches today, based directly on Daniel's practice of abstaining from rich food while continuing to eat basic nourishment.
thenVsNow.now: The 'Daniel Fast' is one of the most commonly practiced fasts in churches today, based directly on Daniel's practice of abstaining from rich food while continuing to eat basic nourishment.

verses.text: In the twenty-fourth day of the first month, as I was by the side of the great river, which is Hiddekel,
verses.explainer: On the twenty-fourth day of the first month, Daniel was standing beside the great Tigris River when the vision came. The specific date and location ground the supernatural experience in concrete reality.

verses.text: I lifted up my eyes, and looked, and behold, there was a man clothed in linen, whose thighs were adorned with pure gold of Uphaz.
verses.explainer: Daniel looked up and saw a man dressed in linen with a belt of fine gold around his waist. The description of this figure -- linen, gold, and overwhelming radiance -- is remarkably similar to John's description of the risen Christ in Revelation 1.

crossReferences.connection: John saw someone 'dressed in a robe reaching down to his feet with a golden sash around his chest' -- strikingly similar to Daniel's vision.

verses.text: His body also was like the beryl, and his face as the appearance of lightning, and his eyes as flaming torches. His arms and his feet were like burnished brass. The voice of his words was like the voice of a multitude.
verses.explainer: His body was like topaz, his face like lightning, his eyes like flaming torches, his arms and legs like burnished bronze, and his voice like the sound of a multitude. Every detail conveys overwhelming power and glory.

crossReferences.connection: Ezekiel's vision of God included a figure with the appearance of glowing metal and fire -- similar imagery.
Jesus's appearance in Revelation shares multiple details: eyes like blazing fire, face shining like the sun, voice like rushing waters.

verses.text: I, Daniel, alone saw the vision; for the men who were with me didn’t see the vision; but a great quaking fell on them, and they fled to hide themselves.
verses.explainer: Only Daniel saw the vision; the men with him did not see it but were overcome with terror and fled. The parallel with Paul's Damascus Road experience (Acts 9:7) is notable -- companions sense something but do not see the vision.

crossReferences.connection: The men with Paul 'heard the sound but did not see anyone' -- the same pattern of companions partially perceiving but not fully sharing the revelatory experience.

verses.text: So I was left alone, and saw this great vision. No strength remained in me; for my comeliness was turned in me into corruption, and I retained no strength.
verses.explainer: Daniel was left alone with this great vision. His strength left him, his face turned deathly pale, and he was helpless. Encountering the divine drains human strength completely.

verses.text: Yet I heard the voice of his words. When I heard the voice of his words, then I fell into a deep sleep on my face, with my face toward the ground.
verses.explainer: When Daniel heard the voice, he fell into a deep sleep face down on the ground. The same response as chapter 8 -- divine encounter overwhelms human consciousness.

crossReferences.connection: 'When I saw him, I fell at his feet as though dead.'

verses.text: Behold, a hand touched me, which set me on my knees and on the palms of my hands.
verses.explainer: A hand touched Daniel and set him trembling on his hands and knees. The divine touch partially revives him but he remains on the ground, shaking.

verses.text: He said to me, Daniel, you greatly beloved man, understand the words that I speak to you, and stand upright; for I have been sent to you, now. When he had spoken this word to me, I stood trembling.
verses.explainer: The angel addresses Daniel as a man 'greatly loved' and tells him to stand up. The affirmation of God's love precedes the commission -- Daniel needs to know he is treasured before he can receive the difficult message.

verses.text: Then he said to me, “Don’t be afraid, Daniel; for from the first day that you set your heart to understand, and to humble yourself before your God, your words were heard. I have come for your words’ sake.
verses.explainer: From the first day Daniel set his mind to understand and humble himself before God, his words were heard. The angel was sent in response to Daniel's prayer on day one -- but was delayed 21 days.

crossReferences.connection: Gabriel also told Daniel his prayer was answered immediately in chapter 9.

thenVsNow.then: Delayed answers to prayer do not mean unheard prayers. God responded immediately; the delivery was delayed by spiritual opposition.
thenVsNow.now: Delayed answers to prayer do not mean unheard prayers. God responded immediately; the delivery was delayed by spiritual opposition.

verses.text: But the prince of the kingdom of Persia withstood me twenty-one days; but, behold, Michael, one of the chief princes, came to help me because I remained there with the kings of Persia.
verses.explainer: The prince of the kingdom of Persia opposed the angel for 21 days. Then Michael, one of the chief princes, came to help. This verse reveals a layer of reality invisible to human eyes: angelic and demonic forces fighting over the fate of nations.

culturalNotes.title: Spiritual Warfare
culturalNotes.note: This passage is the most explicit biblical text about spiritual beings exerting influence over nations. The 'prince of Persia' is not a human ruler but a spiritual power associated with the Persian Empire. The concept that nations have spiritual patrons (both good and evil) was widespread in the ancient world but is uniquely developed here.

crossReferences.connection: 'Our struggle is not against flesh and blood, but against the rulers, against the authorities, against the powers of this dark world.'
Michael the archangel disputed with the devil -- confirming Michael's role in spiritual warfare.

verses.text: Now I have come to make you understand what will happen to your people in the latter days; for the vision is yet for many days.”
verses.explainer: The angel has come to help Daniel understand what will happen to his people in the future. The vision concerns days yet to come -- the sweep of future history.

verses.text: When he had spoken these words to me, I set my face toward the ground, and was mute.
verses.explainer: While the angel spoke, Daniel looked toward the ground and was unable to speak. The overwhelm continues -- Daniel, the most gifted communicator in the Babylonian court, is struck speechless.

verses.text: Behold, one in the likeness of the sons of men touched my lips. Then I opened my mouth, and spoke and said to him who stood before me, “My lord, by reason of the vision my sorrows have overtaken me, and I retain no strength.
verses.explainer: Someone who looked like a man touched Daniel's lips, and he was able to speak again. He confessed to the angel: the vision has overwhelmed him and he has no strength.

crossReferences.connection: A seraph touched Isaiah's lips with a coal from the altar, enabling him to speak -- a similar pattern of divine enablement.

verses.text: For how can the servant of this my lord talk with this my lord? For as for me, immediately there remained no strength in me. There was no breath left in me.”
verses.explainer: Daniel asks how he, a mere servant, can talk with someone so exalted. He has no strength and can barely breathe. The gap between the human and the divine is vividly experienced.

verses.text: Then one like the appearance of a man touched me again, and he strengthened me.
verses.explainer: The figure touched Daniel again and strengthened him. Three touches in this chapter (vv.10, 16, 18) gradually restore Daniel's ability to function. Each touch brings more strength.

verses.text:
He said, “Greatly beloved man, don’t be afraid. Peace be to you. Be strong. Yes, be strong.”
When he spoke to me, I was strengthened, and said, “Let my lord speak; for you have strengthened me.”
verses.explainer: The angel says: 'Peace to you! Be strong! Be very strong!' When he spoke, Daniel was strengthened and said, 'Speak, my lord, since you have strengthened me.' The combination of divine peace and strength enables Daniel to receive the difficult vision.

verses.text: Then he said, “Do you know why I have come to you? Now I will return to fight with the prince of Persia. When I go out, behold, the prince of Greece will come.
verses.explainer: The angel explains that he must return to fight the prince of Persia, and then the prince of Greece will come. The spiritual conflict continues -- the angel's work is not finished.

verses.text: But I will tell you that which is inscribed in the writing of truth. There is no one who holds with me against these, but Michael your prince.
verses.explainer: But first, the angel will tell Daniel what is written in the Book of Truth. Only Michael, Israel's prince, supports the angel in this battle. The 'Book of Truth' suggests that future events are already recorded in heaven.

crossReferences.connection: The sealed scroll in heaven that only the Lamb can open may be related to this 'Book of Truth.'

explainer.summary: Chapter 11 contains the most detailed prophecy in the Bible, describing the conflicts between the Ptolemaic (Egyptian) and Seleucid (Syrian) kingdoms after Alexander's death, culminating in the career of Antiochus IV Epiphanes. Many of the specific details match known historical events with remarkable precision. The chapter then shifts to describe a final 'king of the north' whose actions go beyond anything Antiochus did, likely pointing to an end-times figure.
themes: The detailed unfolding of history under God's sovereignty
The wars of the kings of the north and south
The final king and the end of the age
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 11 provides the detailed content of the vision introduced in chapter 10. It traces the political history of the region from Persia through Greece and its successors, with special focus on the persecutor Antiochus Epiphanes and his end-times counterpart.

wordStudies.meaning: Despised, scorned, worthless. Someone regarded with contempt by others.
wordStudies.significance: Antiochus IV is described as a 'contemptible person' who was not given royal honor but seized power through intrigue -- he was not the legitimate heir to the throne.

wordStudies.meaning: A detestable thing, an abomination. Used specifically for idolatrous objects that desecrate sacred space.
wordStudies.significance: The 'abomination of desolation' (v.31) refers to the pagan altar Antiochus erected in the Temple. Jesus used this same phrase to describe a future desecration.

verses.text: “As for me, in the first year of Darius the Mede, I stood up to confirm and strengthen him.
verses.explainer: The angel tells Daniel that he supported and protected Darius the Mede in his first year. Even the transition of empires from Babylon to Persia involved angelic activity behind the scenes.

verses.text: “Now will I show you the truth. Behold, three more kings will stand up in Persia; and the fourth will be far richer than all of them. When he has grown strong through his riches, he will stir up all against the realm of Greece.
verses.explainer: Three more kings will arise in Persia, then a fourth who will be far richer than all the others and will stir up everyone against the kingdom of Greece. This likely refers to Xerxes I, who invaded Greece in 480 BC.

culturalNotes.title: Xerxes' Invasion of Greece
culturalNotes.note: Xerxes I (486-465 BC) assembled one of the largest armies in ancient history to invade Greece. Despite initial success, he was defeated at the naval battle of Salamis and the land battle of Plataea. This invasion sowed the seeds of Greek revenge that Alexander later fulfilled.

verses.text: A mighty king will stand up, who will rule with great dominion, and do according to his will.
verses.explainer: Then a mighty king will arise who rules with great power and does as he pleases. This is Alexander the Great, whose conquests (334-323 BC) changed the world.

crossReferences.connection: The same conquest described through the goat imagery in chapter 8.

verses.text: When he stands up, his kingdom will be broken, and will be divided toward the four winds of the sky, but not to his posterity, nor according to his dominion with which he ruled; for his kingdom will be plucked up, even for others besides these.
verses.explainer: After he appears, his empire will be broken up and parceled out toward the four winds of heaven. It will not go to his descendants, nor will it have the power he wielded. Alexander's empire was divided among his generals, not his children.

crossReferences.connection: The great horn broken and four horns growing in its place -- the same event.

verses.text: “The king of the south will be strong. One of his princes will become stronger than him, and have dominion. His dominion will be a great dominion.
verses.explainer: The king of the south (Ptolemy I of Egypt) will become strong, but one of his commanders (Seleucus I) will become even stronger and establish his own kingdom. The Ptolemaic and Seleucid dynasties will dominate the following prophecy.

culturalNotes.title: Kings of North and South
culturalNotes.note: 'King of the south' refers to the Ptolemaic dynasty in Egypt. 'King of the north' refers to the Seleucid dynasty in Syria/Mesopotamia. Israel lay between them and was fought over repeatedly. The detailed accuracy of verses 5-35 has led some scholars to date the book to after these events occurred.

verses.text: At the end of years they will join themselves together; and the daughter of the king of the south will come to the king of the north to make an agreement; but she will not retain the strength of her arm. He will also not stand, nor will his arm; but she will be given up, with those who brought her, and he who became the father of her, and he who strengthened her in those times.
verses.explainer: After some years, they will make an alliance through a marriage, but the alliance will not last and she and her attendants will be given up. This describes the marriage of Berenice (Ptolemy II's daughter) to Antiochus II, which ended in her murder.

verses.text: “But out of a shoot from her roots one will stand up in his place, who will come to the army, and will enter into the fortress of the king of the north, and will deal against them, and will prevail.
verses.explainer: A branch from her roots will arise, attack the king of the north's fortress, and prevail. This is Ptolemy III, who invaded the Seleucid Empire in revenge for his sister Berenice's murder.

verses.text: He will also carry their gods, with their molten images, and with their goodly vessels of silver and of gold, captive into Egypt. He will refrain some years from the king of the north.
verses.explainer: He will carry off their gods, metal images, and valuable articles to Egypt and will leave the king of the north alone for some years. Ptolemy III's successful campaign brought immense plunder back to Egypt.

verses.text: He will come into the realm of the king of the south, but he will return into his own land.
verses.explainer: The king of the north will invade the realm of the king of the south but will retreat to his own country. The Seleucid counterattack failed.

verses.text: His sons will wage war, and will assemble a multitude of great forces, which will come on, and overflow, and pass through. They will return and wage war, even to his fortress.
verses.explainer: His sons will prepare for war and assemble a great army that sweeps through like a flood. The Seleucid forces under Antiochus III launched major campaigns against Ptolemaic territories.

verses.text: “The king of the south will be moved with anger, and will come out and fight with him, even with the king of the north. He will send out a great multitude, and the multitude will be given into his hand.
verses.explainer: The king of the south (Ptolemy IV) will march out in a rage and fight the king of the north (Antiochus III), who will raise a large army that will be defeated. This is the Battle of Raphia in 217 BC.

verses.text: The multitude will be lifted up, and his heart will be exalted. He will cast down tens of thousands, but he won’t prevail.
verses.explainer: When the army is carried off, the king of the south will be filled with pride but will not remain triumphant despite slaughtering many thousands. Ptolemy IV won the battle but failed to capitalize on his victory.

verses.text: The king of the north will return, and will send out a multitude greater than the former. He will come on at the end of the times, even of years, with a great army and with much substance.
verses.explainer: The king of the north will muster another army, larger than the first, and after several years will advance with a huge army and much equipment. Antiochus III rebuilt his forces for a second campaign.

verses.text: “In those times many will stand up against the king of the south. Also the children of the violent among your people will lift themselves up to establish the vision; but they will fall.
verses.explainer: In those times many will rise against the king of the south. Violent men among Daniel's own people will rebel to fulfill the vision, but they will fail. Some Jews allied with the Seleucids against Ptolemaic Egypt during this period.

verses.text: So the king of the north will come and cast up a mound, and take a well-fortified city. The forces of the south won’t stand, neither will his chosen people, neither will there be any strength to stand.
verses.explainer: The king of the north will come and build siege ramps and capture a fortified city. The forces of the south will be powerless to resist. Antiochus III captured the strategic fortress of Sidon in 198 BC.

verses.text: But he who comes against him will do according to his own will, and no one will stand before him. He will stand in the glorious land, and destruction will be in his hand.
verses.explainer: The invader will do as he pleases in the Beautiful Land (Israel), with the power to destroy. Antiochus III's conquest of Palestine transferred it from Ptolemaic to Seleucid control.

verses.text: He will set his face to come with the strength of his whole kingdom, and with him equitable conditions. He will perform them. He will give him the daughter of women, to corrupt her; but she will not stand, and won’t be for him.
verses.explainer: He will make an alliance through a daughter given in marriage, but the plan will not succeed. Antiochus III gave his daughter Cleopatra I to Ptolemy V, hoping she would be loyal to Syria, but she sided with her husband.

verses.text: After this he will turn his face to the islands, and will take many; but a prince will cause the reproach offered by him to cease. Yes, moreover, he will cause his reproach to turn on him.
verses.explainer: He will turn his attention to the coastlands and take many of them, but a commander will put an end to his insolence. Antiochus III expanded into Asia Minor and Greece but was defeated by the Roman general Scipio at the Battle of Magnesia in 190 BC.

verses.text: Then he will turn his face toward the fortresses of his own land; but he will stumble and fall, and won’t be found.
verses.explainer: He will stumble and fall, to be seen no more. Antiochus III died in 187 BC while trying to plunder a temple to pay his war debts to Rome.

verses.text: “Then one who will cause a tax collector to pass through the kingdom to maintain its glory will stand up in his place; but within few days he shall be destroyed, not in anger, and not in battle.
verses.explainer: His successor will send out a tax collector but will be destroyed within a few days, not in anger or battle. Seleucus IV (187-175 BC) was known mainly for his heavy taxation and was assassinated by his own minister.

verses.text: “In his place a contemptible person will stand up, to whom they had not given the honor of the kingdom; but he will come in time of security, and will obtain the kingdom by flatteries.
verses.explainer: He will be succeeded by a contemptible person not given the honor of royalty. He will seize the kingdom through intrigue. This is Antiochus IV Epiphanes, who was not the legitimate heir but seized power by political manipulation.

verses.text: The overwhelming forces will be overwhelmed from before him, and will be broken. Yes, also the prince of the covenant.
verses.explainer: A massive army will be swept away before him and destroyed, along with a prince of the covenant. The 'prince of the covenant' may be the high priest Onias III, who was murdered in 171 BC.

verses.text: After the treaty made with him he will work deceitfully; for he will come up, and will become strong, with a small people.
verses.explainer: After alliances are made with him, he will act deceitfully and with only a few people will rise to power. Antiochus gained power through deception and a small group of supporters.

verses.text: In time of security he will come even on the fattest places of the province. He will do that which his fathers have not done, nor his fathers’ fathers. He will scatter among them prey, plunder, and substance. Yes, he will devise his plans against the strongholds, even for a time.
verses.explainer: When the richest provinces feel secure, he will invade them and distribute plunder, loot, and wealth among his followers. He will plot against strongholds -- but only for a time. His generosity was strategic, buying loyalty with other people's wealth.

verses.text: “He will stir up his power and his courage against the king of the south with a great army; and the king of the south will wage war in battle with an exceedingly great and mighty army; but he won’t stand; for they will devise plans against him.
verses.explainer: He will stir up his strength and courage against the king of the south with a large army. The king of the south will wage war with a very large and powerful army but will not succeed because of plots devised against him.

verses.text: Yes, those who eat of his dainties will destroy him, and his army will be swept away. Many will fall down slain.
verses.explainer: Those who eat at his table will try to destroy him; his army will be swept away and many will fall. Internal treachery will undermine the king of the south's position.

verses.text: As for both these kings, their hearts will be to do mischief, and they will speak lies at one table; but it won’t prosper, for the end will still be at the appointed time.
verses.explainer: The two kings, with their hearts bent on evil, will sit at the same table and lie to each other. But it will accomplish nothing because the end will come at the appointed time. Even scheming rulers cannot alter God's timeline.

thenVsNow.then: The image of two rulers lying to each other across a negotiating table is timeless. Diplomacy without integrity is futile.
thenVsNow.now: The image of two rulers lying to each other across a negotiating table is timeless. Diplomacy without integrity is futile.

verses.text: Then he will return into his land with great wealth. His heart will be against the holy covenant. He will take action, and return to his own land.
verses.explainer: The king of the north will return to his country with great wealth. His heart will be set against the holy covenant, and he will take action against it before returning home. On his return from Egypt in 169 BC, Antiochus looted the Jerusalem Temple.

crossReferences.connection: The historical account of Antiochus entering the Temple and stripping it of its gold, silver, and sacred objects.

verses.text: “He will return at the appointed time, and come into the south; but it won’t be in the latter time as it was in the former.
verses.explainer: At the appointed time, he will invade the south again, but this time it will not turn out as it did before. His second Egyptian campaign will end differently from the first.

verses.text: For ships of Kittim will come against him. Therefore he will be grieved, and will return, and have indignation against the holy covenant, and will take action. He will even return, and have regard to those who forsake the holy covenant.
verses.explainer: Ships of Kittim (Rome) will oppose him, and he will lose heart. He will turn back and vent his fury against the holy covenant. A Roman envoy (Gaius Popillius Laenas) drew a circle around Antiochus and demanded he leave Egypt immediately. Humiliated, Antiochus took out his rage on Jerusalem.

culturalNotes.title: The Circle of Popillius
culturalNotes.note: In 168 BC, the Roman senator Popillius Laenas confronted Antiochus in Egypt. He drew a circle around the king in the sand and demanded a decision before Antiochus stepped out of it. This humiliation before his own troops drove Antiochus to redirect his fury toward the Jews.

verses.text: “Forces will stand on his part, and they will profane the sanctuary, even the fortress, and will take away the continual burnt offering. Then they will set up the abomination that makes desolate.
verses.explainer: His armed forces will desecrate the Temple fortress, abolish the daily sacrifice, and set up the abomination of desolation. In December 167 BC, Antiochus erected a pagan altar (likely to Zeus Olympios) in the Jerusalem Temple and sacrificed pigs on it.

crossReferences.connection: 'On the fifteenth day of Chislev, they erected an abomination of desolation upon the altar of burnt offering.'
Jesus pointed to this passage when warning of a future desecration: 'When you see the abomination of desolation spoken of by the prophet Daniel.'

verses.text: He will corrupt those who do wickedly against the covenant by flatteries; but the people who know their God will be strong, and take action.
verses.explainer: With flattery he will corrupt those who violate the covenant, but the people who know their God will firmly resist him. Two responses to persecution: some compromise, others stand firm. Knowing God personally makes the difference.

crossReferences.connection: Many Israelites 'chose to serve the king's decree' and abandoned the covenant -- the compromisers described here.
Mattathias cried: 'Let everyone who is zealous for the law follow me!' -- the resisters described here.

thenVsNow.then: In every era of persecution, some compromise and some resist. The difference, Daniel says, is knowing God personally.
thenVsNow.now: In every era of persecution, some compromise and some resist. The difference, Daniel says, is knowing God personally.

verses.text: “Those who are wise among the people will instruct many; yet they will fall by the sword and by flame, by captivity and by plunder, many days.
verses.explainer: Those who are wise will instruct many, though they will fall by sword, flame, captivity, and plunder for a time. The faithful will suffer -- wisdom and faithfulness do not guarantee personal safety.

crossReferences.connection: The 'heroes of faith' who were tortured, persecuted, and killed -- many of whom lived during this very period.

verses.text: Now when they fall, they will be helped with a little help; but many will join themselves to them with flatteries.
verses.explainer: When they fall, they will receive a little help, and many who are not sincere will join them. The 'little help' may refer to the Maccabean revolt, which provided relief but was not the ultimate deliverance.

culturalNotes.title: The Maccabean Revolt
culturalNotes.note: In 167 BC, the priest Mattathias and his sons (the Maccabees) launched a guerrilla war against Antiochus. By 164 BC, they had recaptured and rededicated the Temple (celebrated as Hanukkah). However, the verse calls this only 'a little help,' suggesting the ultimate deliverance is still future.

verses.text: Some of those who are wise will fall, to refine them, and to purify, and to make them white, even to the time of the end; because it is yet for the time appointed.
verses.explainer: Some of the wise will stumble, to be refined, purified, and made spotless until the time of the end, for it will still come at the appointed time. Suffering serves a purifying purpose, and it has a divinely appointed end date.

crossReferences.connection: 'You may have had to suffer grief in all kinds of trials. These have come so that your faith may be proved genuine.'

verses.text: “The king will do according to his will. He will exalt himself, and magnify himself above every god, and will speak marvelous things against the God of gods. He will prosper until the indignation is accomplished; for that which is determined will be done.
verses.explainer: The king will do as he pleases. He will exalt and magnify himself above every god and speak monstrous things against the God of gods. He will prosper until the time of wrath is completed. Many scholars see a shift here from Antiochus to an end-times figure whose actions exceed what Antiochus did.

crossReferences.connection: 'The man of lawlessness... will oppose and will exalt himself over everything that is called God or is worshiped, so that he sets himself up in God's temple.'
The beast 'opened his mouth to blaspheme God, and to slander his name.'

verses.text: He won’t regard the gods of his fathers, or the desire of women, or regard any god; for he will magnify himself above all.
verses.explainer: He will show no regard for the gods of his ancestors or the desire of women, nor for any god, because he will magnify himself above all. He rejects all worship except worship of himself.

verses.text: But in his place he will honor the god of fortresses. He will honor a god whom his fathers didn’t know with gold, silver, and with precious stones and pleasant things.
verses.explainer: Instead of them, he will honor a god of fortresses -- a god unknown to his ancestors. He will honor it with gold, silver, precious stones, and costly gifts. This ruler worships power itself -- military might as his god.

verses.text: He will deal with the strongest fortresses by the help of a foreign god. He will increase with glory whoever acknowledges him. He will cause them to rule over many, and will divide the land for a price.
verses.explainer: He will attack the mightiest fortresses with the help of a foreign god. Those who acknowledge him will receive great honor, and he will distribute the land at a price. Power and loyalty become a transactional system.

verses.text: “At the time of the end the king of the south will contend with him; and the king of the north will come against him like a whirlwind, with chariots, with horsemen, and with many ships. He will enter into the countries, and will overflow and pass through.
verses.explainer: At the time of the end, the king of the south will engage him in battle, and the king of the north will storm out with chariots, cavalry, and many ships. He will invade many countries and sweep through them like a flood.

thenVsNow.then: The pattern of military conquest and counter-conquest described here has repeated throughout history, with the specific geography of the Middle East remaining a focal point of global conflict.
thenVsNow.now: The pattern of military conquest and counter-conquest described here has repeated throughout history, with the specific geography of the Middle East remaining a focal point of global conflict.

verses.text: He will enter also into the glorious land, and many countries will be overthrown; but these will be delivered out of his hand: Edom, Moab, and the chief of the children of Ammon.
verses.explainer: He will invade the Beautiful Land (Israel). Many countries will fall, but Edom, Moab, and the leaders of Ammon will be delivered from his hand. Israel will not be spared, though some neighboring territories will escape.

verses.text: He will also stretch out his hand on the countries. The land of Egypt won’t escape.
verses.explainer: He will extend his power over many countries; even Egypt will not escape. His dominion will expand across the ancient world.

verses.text: But he will have power over the treasures of gold and of silver, and over all the precious things of Egypt. The Libyans and the Ethiopians will be at his steps.
verses.explainer: He will gain control of the treasures of gold and silver and all the riches of Egypt, with Libya and Ethiopia submitting to him. His economic domination will be complete.

verses.text: But news out of the east and out of the north will trouble him; and he will go out with great fury to destroy and utterly to sweep away many.
verses.explainer: But reports from the east and north will alarm him, and he will set out in a great rage to destroy many. External threats will provoke him to further violence.

verses.text: He will plant the tents of his palace between the sea and the glorious holy mountain; yet he will come to his end, and no one will help him.
verses.explainer: He will pitch his royal tents between the seas at the beautiful holy mountain. Yet he will come to his end, and no one will help him. Despite his power, his end is certain and he will die without allies -- a solitary, ignominious end for a world conqueror.

crossReferences.connection: The beast and the false prophet are captured and thrown into the fiery lake -- the ultimate fulfillment of this tyrant's end.

explainer.summary: The final chapter brings the book to its climax with the promise of resurrection, final judgment, and ultimate vindication of the faithful. Michael will arise to protect God's people during an unprecedented time of distress. The dead will be raised -- some to everlasting life, others to everlasting contempt. Daniel is told to seal the book until the time of the end, and he is promised that he will rest and rise to receive his allotted inheritance at the end of days.
themes: The sealing of prophecy until the end
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 12 concludes both the final vision (chapters 10-12) and the entire book. It provides the ultimate answer to the suffering described throughout Daniel: there is a resurrection, there is a judgment, and the faithful will be vindicated. Daniel's personal story ends with a promise that he will stand in his allotted place at the end.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To wake up, to rouse from sleep
wordStudies.meaning: To wake up, to rouse from sleep. Used metaphorically for resurrection -- death is described as sleep from which the dead will be awakened.
wordStudies.significance: The dead 'sleeping in the dust of the earth' will 'awake' -- this is the clearest statement of bodily resurrection in the Old Testament.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To shine, to radiate light, to gleam like the brig
wordStudies.meaning: To shine, to radiate light, to gleam like the brightness of the sky. Implies brilliant, glorious radiance.
wordStudies.significance: Those who are wise will 'shine like the brightness of the heavens' -- their faithfulness will be rewarded with eternal glory.

verses.text: “At that time Michael will stand up, the great prince who stands for the children of your people; and there will be a time of trouble, such as never was since there was a nation even to that same time. At that time your people will be delivered, everyone who is found written in the book.
verses.explainer: At that time Michael, the great prince who protects Daniel's people, will arise. There will be a time of distress such as has not happened since the beginning of nations. But everyone whose name is found written in the book will be delivered.

crossReferences.connection: Jesus quoted this verse: 'For then there will be great distress, unequaled from the beginning of the world until now.'
'Michael and his angels fought against the dragon' -- Michael's role in the final spiritual battle.

thenVsNow.then: The 'book of life' concept -- a divine registry of those who belong to God -- appears in both Testaments and assures believers that their identity is secure regardless of earthly circumstances.
thenVsNow.now: The 'book of life' concept -- a divine registry of those who belong to God -- appears in both Testaments and assures believers that their identity is secure regardless of earthly circumstances.

verses.text: Many of those who sleep in the dust of the earth will awake, some to everlasting life, and some to shame and everlasting contempt.
verses.explainer: Multitudes who sleep in the dust of the earth will awake: some to everlasting life, others to shame and everlasting contempt. This is the Old Testament's clearest statement of bodily resurrection and dual eternal destinies.

crossReferences.connection: Jesus taught: 'A time is coming when all who are in their graves will hear his voice and come out -- those who have done what is good will rise to live, and those who have done what is evil will rise to be condemned.'
Paul affirmed 'there will be a resurrection of both the righteous and the wicked.'

thenVsNow.then: The belief in bodily resurrection is foundational to both Judaism and Christianity. This verse is the primary Old Testament basis for that belief.
thenVsNow.now: The belief in bodily resurrection is foundational to both Judaism and Christianity. This verse is the primary Old Testament basis for that belief.

verses.text: Those who are wise will shine as the brightness of the expanse. Those who turn many to righteousness will shine as the stars forever and ever.
verses.explainer: Those who are wise will shine like the brightness of the heavens, and those who lead many to righteousness, like the stars for ever and ever. Faithfulness and wisdom will be rewarded with eternal glory.

crossReferences.connection: Jesus said: 'Then the righteous will shine like the sun in the kingdom of their Father.'
'You shine among them like stars in the sky.'

thenVsNow.then: The promise that the faithful will shine eternally gives perspective on temporary suffering. What seems insignificant now may have everlasting significance.
thenVsNow.now: The promise that the faithful will shine eternally gives perspective on temporary suffering. What seems insignificant now may have everlasting significance.

verses.text: But you, Daniel, shut up the words, and seal the book, even to the time of the end. Many will run back and forth, and knowledge will be increased.”
verses.explainer: Daniel is told to close up and seal the words of the scroll until the time of the end. Many will go here and there to increase knowledge. The sealing preserves the prophecy for future generations who will need it most.

crossReferences.connection: In contrast, John is told NOT to seal up his prophecy 'because the time is near' -- Daniel's sealed prophecy is now being unsealed.

verses.text: Then I, Daniel, looked, and behold, two others stood, one on the river bank on this side, and the other on the river bank on that side.
verses.explainer: Daniel looked and saw two others standing on opposite banks of the river. Angelic witnesses frame the final exchange of the book.

verses.text: One said to the man clothed in linen, who was above the waters of the river, “How long will it be to the end of these wonders?”
verses.explainer: One of them asked the man clothed in linen, who was above the waters of the river: 'How long until these astonishing things are fulfilled?' The same question that echoes throughout the prophets: how long?

crossReferences.connection: The martyrs under the altar cry out: 'How long, Sovereign Lord, until you judge the inhabitants of the earth?'

verses.text: I heard the man clothed in linen, who was above the waters of the river, when he held up his right hand and his left hand to heaven, and swore by him who lives forever that it will be for a time, times, and a half; and when they have finished breaking in pieces the power of the holy people, all these things will be finished.
verses.explainer: The man in linen raised both hands toward heaven and swore by Him who lives forever that it will be for a time, times and half a time. When the power of the holy people has been finally broken, all these things will be completed.

crossReferences.connection: The same 'time, times and half a time' (3.5 years) mentioned in the vision of the four beasts.
The woman is nourished for 'a time, times, and half a time' -- the same period of divine protection during persecution.

verses.text: I heard, but I didn’t understand. Then I said, “My lord, what will be the outcome of these things?”
verses.explainer: Daniel heard but did not understand. He asked: 'My lord, what will the outcome of all this be?' Even the prophet who received the visions did not fully comprehend their meaning.

crossReferences.connection: 'Even the prophets who prophesied about the grace that was to come to you searched intently and with the greatest care, trying to find out the time and circumstances.'

verses.text: He said, “Go your way, Daniel; for the words are shut up and sealed until the time of the end.
verses.explainer: The reply: 'Go your way, Daniel, because the words are rolled up and sealed until the time of the end.' Daniel is told that full understanding will not come in his lifetime -- it is reserved for a future generation.

verses.text: Many will purify themselves, and make themselves white, and be refined; but the wicked will do wickedly; and none of the wicked will understand; but those who are wise will understand.
verses.explainer: Many will be purified, made spotless, and refined, but the wicked will continue to be wicked. None of the wicked will understand, but those who are wise will understand. Suffering purifies the faithful; the wicked remain unchanged.

crossReferences.connection: 'Let the one who does wrong continue to do wrong; let the one who is righteous continue to do right.'

verses.text: “From the time that the continual burnt offering is taken away, and the abomination that makes desolate set up, there will be one thousand two hundred ninety days.
verses.explainer: From the time that the daily sacrifice is abolished and the abomination that causes desolation is set up, there will be 1,290 days. This specific number extends slightly beyond the three and a half years, possibly accounting for the period between the desecration and the full restoration.

verses.text: Blessed is he who waits, and comes to the one thousand three hundred thirty-five days.
verses.explainer: Blessed is the one who waits for and reaches the end of the 1,335 days. An additional 45 days beyond the 1,290 -- those who endure to the very end receive a special blessing.

crossReferences.connection: 'The one who stands firm to the end will be saved.'

thenVsNow.then: The promise of blessing for those who persevere through the full duration of suffering is a powerful encouragement for anyone enduring a season that seems to never end.
thenVsNow.now: The promise of blessing for those who persevere through the full duration of suffering is a powerful encouragement for anyone enduring a season that seems to never end.

verses.text: “But go you your way until the end; for you will rest, and will stand in your inheritance at the end of the days.”
verses.explainer: The book's final verse is addressed personally to Daniel: 'Go your way till the end. You will rest, and then at the end of the days you will rise to receive your allotted inheritance.' Daniel is promised personal resurrection and an eternal reward. After a lifetime of faithful service through exile, the final word is hope.

crossReferences.connection: Paul echoed this: 'I have fought the good fight, I have finished the race, I have kept the faith. Now there is in store for me the crown of righteousness.'
'These were all commended for their faith, yet none of them received what had been promised, since God had planned something better for us.'

thenVsNow.then: The book ends with the promise that death is not the end. Daniel will 'rest' (die) and then 'rise' (be resurrected) to receive his inheritance. This is the hope that sustains every person of faith who serves God in difficult circumstances.
thenVsNow.now: The book ends with the promise that death is not the end. Daniel will 'rest' (die) and then 'rise' (be resurrected) to receive his inheritance. This is the hope that sustains every person of faith who serves God in difficult circumstances.
